title: Number to Words Converter — English Word Form
shortDescription: Convert any number up to 999 quadrillion into English words. Shows the full word form, comma-formatted number, and word count.
description: Type any integer or decimal (up to 15 digits) and get the complete English word representation instantly. Handles negatives, decimals, and numbers up to 999 quadrillion.
metaDescription: Free number to words converter. Converts integers and decimals up to 999 quadrillion into English word form. Supports negatives and decimal points.

note.englishOnly: Output is always in English, regardless of interface language.

error.outOfRange: Enter a number up to 999 quadrillion (15 digits). Decimals allowed.

sections.heading: A brief history of number words
paragraphs: Long before written numerals existed, humans named numbers with words. Proto-Indo-European roots such as *oynos (one) and *dwoh₁ (two) spread across languages that eventually became English, Latin, Greek, Sanskrit, and dozens more. The Old English forms 'ān', 'twā', 'þrīe' evolved steadily into the modern one, two, three you use today. Surprisingly, the names for large groups — hundred, thousand — have equally ancient roots, with 'hundred' tracing to Proto-Germanic *hunda-raþa meaning 'ten-tens count'.

The -illion naming convention used in English today follows a two-track history. The 'short scale' (in use in the US and UK since 1974) defines billion as 10⁹, trillion as 10¹², and so on, each step multiplying by 1000. The 'long scale' — still used in some European countries — defines billion as 10¹², a million million. This converter uses the short scale: one billion = 1,000,000,000.

sections.heading: How number naming systems work
paragraphs: English names numbers in groups of three digits (ones, thousands, millions, billions, …). Each group of three is spoken as a hundreds + tens + ones combination, followed by the group's magnitude word. So 1,234,567 breaks into 1 (million) + 234 (thousand) + 567. Each chunk gets spoken independently: 'one million two hundred thirty-four thousand five hundred sixty-seven'. Hyphens appear between tens and ones (twenty-one, ninety-nine) but not between hundreds and the rest.

Decimals are read digit by digit after the word 'point'. So 3.14 becomes 'three point one four', not 'three point fourteen'. This is the standard spoken form in English. Some formal documents read decimals as fractions ('three and fourteen hundredths'), but the digit-by-digit form is more broadly understood and what this tool produces.

sections.heading: Practical uses for number-to-words conversion
paragraphs: Legal and financial documents routinely write amounts in both digits and words — bank checks, contracts, and invoices all use phrases like 'one hundred twenty-five dollars and fifty cents' to reduce ambiguity and prevent tampering. Many jurisdictions actually require the word form by law.

Software developers use number-to-words libraries for text-to-speech, accessibility features, and natural language generation. When a screen reader encounters the number 1,000,000 in a financial table, converting it to 'one million' gives hearing-impaired users a much clearer experience. For internationalization engineers, this tool is also a quick sanity check: does your locale's currency formatter match what the spoken form should be?

howTo.name: Type your number
howTo.text: Enter any integer or decimal in the input box. Use a period for the decimal point. Negative numbers are supported with a leading minus sign.

howTo.name: Read the word form
howTo.text: The full English word representation appears instantly below the input. Large numbers are broken into groups: millions, thousands, hundreds, tens, and ones.

howTo.name: Check the formatted number
howTo.text: Below the words you'll also see the number with commas inserted (e.g., 1,234,567) and the total number of words used.

faq.q: How do you write 1,000,000 in words?
faq.a: One million. The word 'million' represents 10⁶ (1 followed by 6 zeros). In short-scale English, 1,000,000,000 is one billion.

faq.q: What is one billion in numbers?
faq.a: In the short scale (used in English-speaking countries since 1974), one billion = 1,000,000,000 (10⁹). In the long scale still used in some countries, one billion = 1,000,000,000,000 (10¹²). This tool uses the short scale.

faq.q: How do you say 1,000,000,000,000 in words?
faq.a: One trillion (short scale). That is 10¹², one thousand billions, or one million millions.

faq.q: Why does 21 have a hyphen (twenty-one) but 121 doesn't?
faq.a: English style guides (AP, Chicago, Merriam-Webster) consistently hyphenate compound tens like twenty-one through ninety-nine, but do not hyphenate 'one hundred twenty-one'. The hyphen marks the tight bond between the tens digit and the ones digit.
Yes. Enter a number with a decimal point (e.g. 3.14) and the tool reads each digit after 'point' individually: 'three point one four'. This is the standard spoken English convention.

faq.q: What is the largest number this tool can convert?
faq.a: 999,999,999,999,999 — that is 999 trillion (technically, nine hundred ninety-nine trillion nine hundred ninety-nine billion nine hundred ninety-nine million nine hundred ninety-nine thousand nine hundred ninety-nine). The limit is 15 digits before the decimal.
Prefix your number with a minus sign. The tool will prepend 'negative' to the word form. For example, -42 becomes 'negative forty-two'.

faq.q: Does this send my data to a server?
faq.a: No. All conversion logic runs in your browser. Nothing you type is transmitted anywhere.
